prompt: A classmate says, 'It was just a joke. You are too sensitive.'
model: It was not funny to me. Do not make that comment again.
why: It rejects minimization, names the boundary, and avoids proving your feelings.

prompt: A coworker says, 'Everyone agrees you are difficult.'
model: Who specifically, and what observable work issue are we discussing?
why: It removes the invisible jury and requests verifiable facts.

prompt: Someone repeats, 'That never happened,' after you quote their message.
model: The message exists. I am not debating whether I read it.
why: It anchors to evidence without entering an endless memory contest.

prompt: An online bully posts a personal insult under your work.
model: Critique the work or leave the thread. Personal attacks will be removed.
why: It protects the space, offers one legitimate route, and states the consequence.

prompt: A friend says, 'If you cared about me, you would prove it right now.'
model: Care is not proved under pressure. My answer is no.
why: It separates affection from coercion and closes the negotiation.

prompt: A relative mocks you, then complains that your boundary is disrespectful.
model: I will speak respectfully, and I expect the same. I am leaving this conversation now.
why: It keeps your standard symmetrical and follows the boundary with action.

prompt: A manager changes an agreed deadline and says you should have known.
model: My written deadline is Friday. Please send the revised priority and deadline in writing.
why: It replaces moving goalposts with a record and a concrete request.

prompt: Someone says, 'Calm down,' whenever you name a problem.
model: I am calm enough to continue. Please answer the specific issue I raised.
why: It refuses tone diversion and returns to the substance.

prompt: A friend says, 'If you cared about me, you would give me your password.'
model: Care does not require surrendering my privacy. I am not sharing my password.
why: It separates affection from control and gives no technical access.

prompt: A manager changes a deadline and later says, 'I told you last week.'
model: My notes show the earlier deadline. Please confirm the new deadline in writing so I can reprioritize.
why: It avoids a memory contest and creates a usable record.

prompt: Someone online says, 'Reply now or I will post our messages.'
model: Do not threaten me. I am preserving this message and ending contact.
why: It names the threat, preserves evidence, and refuses urgency.

prompt: A relative says, 'After everything I have done, you owe me this.'
model: I appreciate what you have done. It does not give you control over this decision.
why: Gratitude and consent are kept separate.

prompt: A classmate says, 'Everyone thinks you are weird.'
model: Who specifically, and what behavior are you asking me to change?
why: It challenges the invisible audience and requests a concrete issue.

prompt: A date says, 'All my exes let me check their phones.'
model: I do not share phone access. If that is required for you, we are not compatible.
why: It states the boundary and makes compatibility the decision point.

prompt: A teammate lists five unrelated mistakes when you ask about one broken promise.
model: We can discuss those separately. Right now I am asking about the promise made on Tuesday.
why: It prevents information flooding from replacing accountability.

prompt: A friend ignores you for days, then says, 'Maybe now you will listen.'
model: Taking space is fine. Using silence to punish me is not. I will talk when communication is respectful.
why: It distinguishes a healthy pause from coercive withdrawal.

prompt: A coworker says, 'You are too emotional to understand this.'
model: Discuss the work issue without commenting on my character.
why: It redirects from a personal attack to the relevant subject.

prompt: Someone says, 'It only happened once, so you cannot be upset.'
model: Frequency does not decide whether I accept the behavior. Do not repeat it.
why: It refuses minimization and sets a forward-looking boundary.
